Method: SearchGym decouples components into abstractions (Dataset, VectorSet, App) and introduces a Compositional Config Algebra for system synthesis. It examines Top-k Cognizance in hybrid retrieval pipelines for optimized ranking and filtering.

Result: SearchGym achieves a 70% Top-100 retrieval rate on an expert-annotated benchmark, emphasizing the balance between generalizability and optimizability in its design.

Conclusion: SearchGym demonstrates the potential for engineering optimization to uncover causal mechanisms in information retrieval across diverse domains, providing opensource tools for robust hybrid search orchestration.

Abstract: The rapid growth of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has created a proliferation of toolkits, yet a fundamental gap remains between experimental prototypes and robust, production-ready systems. We present SearchGym, a modular infrastructure designed for cross-platform benchmarking and hybrid search orchestration. Unlike existing model-centric frameworks, SearchGym decouples data representation, embedding strategies, and retrieval logic into stateful abstractions: Dataset, VectorSet, and App. This separation enables a Compositional Config Algebra, allowing designers to synthesize entire systems from hierarchical configurations while ensuring perfect reproducibility. Moreover, we analyze the "Top-$k$ Cognizance" in hybrid retrieval pipelines, demonstrating that the optimal sequence of semantic ranking and structured filtering is highly dependent on filter strength. Evaluated on the LitSearch expert-annotated benchmark, SearchGym achieves a 70% Top-100 retrieval rate. SearchGym reveals a design tension between generalizability and optimizability, presenting the potential where engineering optimization may serve as a tool for uncovering the causal mechanisms inherent in information retrieval across heterogeneous domains. An open-source implementation of SearchGym is available at: https://github.com/JeromeTH/search-gym

</details>


### [455] [Signal in the Noise: Decoding the Reality of Airline Service Quality with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04404)
*Ahmed Dawoud,Osama El-Shamy,Ahmed Habashy*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The study validates an LLM framework to extract insights from passenger reviews, revealing operational and customer service issues in airlines, especially EgyptAir.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional service quality metrics fail to decode unstructured online feedback that holds critical insights on passenger satisfaction.

Method: Utilized an LLM-based multi-stage pipeline to categorize passenger sentiments from over 16,000 reviews for EgyptAir and Emirates from 2016-2025.

Result: Discovered 'operational perception disconnect' in EgyptAir, with satisfaction dropping despite operational improvements and identified key drivers like poor communication during disruptions.

Conclusion: The LLM framework effectively extracts actionable insights, outperforming traditional methods and benefiting airline/tourism strategies.

Abstract: Traditional service quality metrics often fail to capture the nuanced drivers of passenger satisfaction hidden within unstructured online feedback. This study validates a Large Language Model (LLM) framework designed to extract granular insights from such data. Analyzing over 16,000 TripAdvisor reviews for EgyptAir and Emirates (2016-2025), the study utilizes a multi-stage pipeline to categorize 36 specific service issues. The analysis uncovers a stark "operational perception disconnect" for EgyptAir: despite reported operational improvements, passenger satisfaction plummeted post-2022 (ratings < 2.0). Our approach identified specific drivers missed by conventional metrics-notably poor communication during disruptions and staff conduct-and pinpointed critical sentiment erosion in key tourism markets. These findings confirm the framework's efficacy as a powerful diagnostic tool, surpassing traditional surveys by transforming unstructured passenger voices into actionable strategic intelligence for the airline and tourism sectors.

</details>


### [456] [Still Fresh? Evaluating Temporal Drift in Retrieval Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04532)
*Nathan Kuissi,Suraj Subrahmanyan,Nandan Thakur,Jimmy Lin*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how temporal changes affect the reliability of technical retrieval benchmarks, specifically FreshStack, using snapshots from 2024 and 2025.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the influence of temporal corpus drift on the reliability of retrieval benchmarks, especially in technical domains.

Method: Analyzing FreshStack retrieval performance through corpus snapshots from October 2024 and October 2025, focusing on LangChain queries and comparing retrieval model accuracy.

Result: Most queries from 2024 remain supported in 2025, with relevant documents migrating to competitor repositories. Retrieval model rankings show strong correlation between snapshots.

Conclusion: Retrieval benchmarks judged with evolving corpora remain reliable for evaluation purposes, with minor effects of corpus drift.

Abstract: Information retrieval (IR) benchmarks typically follow the Cranfield paradigm, relying on static and predefined corpora. However, temporal changes in technical corpora, such as API deprecations and code reorganizations, can render existing benchmarks stale. In our work, we investigate how temporal corpus drift affects FreshStack, a retrieval benchmark focused on technical domains. We examine two independent corpus snapshots of FreshStack from October 2024 and October 2025 to answer questions about LangChain. Our analysis shows that all but one query posed in 2024 remain fully supported by the 2025 corpus, as relevant documents "migrate" from LangChain to competitor repositories, such as LlamaIndex. Next, we compare the accuracy of retrieval models on both snapshots and observe only minor shifts in model rankings, with overall strong correlation of up to 0.978 Kendall $τ$ at Recall@50. These results suggest that retrieval benchmarks re-judged with evolving temporal corpora can remain reliable for retrieval evaluation. We publicly release all our artifacts at https://github.com/fresh-stack/driftbench.

</details>


### [457] [DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the R Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04743)
*Maojun Sun,Yue Wu,Yifei Xie,Ruijian Han,Binyan Jiang,Defeng Sun,Yancheng Yuan,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces DARE, a retrieval model that incorporates data distribution into R package retrieval, boosting LLM statistical capabilities and improving R code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with advanced statistical methods and retrieving appropriate tools from R packages, hindering effective automation of data-science workflows.

Method: The authors introduce DARE, a retrieval model integrating data distribution information, alongside components like RPKB (a curated R Package Knowledge Base) and RCodingAgent (an LLM agent for R code generation).

Result: DARE achieves a 93.47% NDCG@10, surpassing existing embedding models by 17% in package retrieval efficacy. Additionally, integrating DARE into RCodingAgent improves LLM performance in statistical tasks.

Conclusion: DARE and its ecosystem improve the effectiveness of LLMs in utilizing R's statistical ecosystem, narrowing the gap between LLM automation and advanced statistical workflows.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents can automate data-science workflows, but many rigorous statistical methods implemented in R remain underused because LLMs struggle with statistical knowledge and tool retrieval. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches focus on function-level semantics and ignore data distribution, producing suboptimal matches. We propose DARE (Distribution-Aware Retrieval Embedding), a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval model that incorporates data distribution information into function representations for R package retrieval. Our main contributions are: (i) RPKB, a curated R Package Knowledge Base derived from 8,191 high-quality CRAN packages; (ii) DARE, an embedding model that fuses distributional features with function metadata to improve retrieval relevance; and (iii) RCodingAgent, an R-oriented LLM agent for reliable R code generation and a suite of statistical analysis tasks for systematically evaluating LLM agents in realistic analytical scenarios. Empirically, DARE achieves an NDCG at 10 of 93.47%, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source embedding models by up to 17% on package retrieval while using substantially fewer parameters. Integrating DARE into RCodingAgent yields significant gains on downstream analysis tasks. This work helps narrow the gap between LLM automation and the mature R statistical ecosystem.

</details>


### [458] [Core-based Hierarchies for Efficient GraphRAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05207)
*Jakir Hossain,Ahmet Erdem Sarıyüce*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: GraphRAG improves Retrieval-Augmented Generation by organizing documents into a deterministic, density-aware knowledge graph, replacing non-reproducible Leiden clustering with k-core decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vector-based methods lack effectiveness in global sensemaking tasks requiring reasoning across multiple documents. Current GraphRAG approaches relying on Leiden clustering fail to provide reproducible results on sparse knowledge graphs.

Method: Replaced Leiden clustering with k-core decomposition for deterministic, density-aware hierarchical graph construction in linear time. Introduced heuristics for connectivity-preserving, size-bounded community formation and a token-budget-aware sampling strategy.

Result: On datasets like financial transcripts and news, the k-core-based GraphRAG showed better comprehensiveness, diversity, and reduced token usage when evaluated against models and judges.

Conclusion: The proposed k-core-based GraphRAG outperforms traditional methods, providing an efficient and effective framework for global sensemaking in LLM-enhanced knowledge tasks.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances large language models by incorporating external knowledge. However, existing vector-based methods often fail on global sensemaking tasks that require reasoning across many documents. GraphRAG addresses this by organizing documents into a knowledge graph with hierarchical communities that can be recursively summarized. Current GraphRAG approaches rely on Leiden clustering for community detection, but we prove that on sparse knowledge graphs, where average degree is constant and most nodes have low degree, modularity optimization admits exponentially many near-optimal partitions, making Leiden-based communities inherently non-reproducible. To address this, we propose replacing Leiden with k-core decomposition, which yields a deterministic, density-aware hierarchy in linear time. We introduce a set of lightweight heuristics that leverage the k-core hierarchy to construct size-bounded, connectivity-preserving communities for retrieval and summarization, along with a token-budget-aware sampling strategy that reduces LLM costs. We evaluate our methods on real-world datasets including financial earnings transcripts, news articles, and podcasts, using three LLMs for answer generation and five independent LLM judges for head-to-head evaluation. Across datasets and models, our approach consistently improves answer comprehensiveness and diversity while reducing token usage, demonstrating that k-core-based GraphRAG is an effective and efficient framework for global sensemaking.

</details>


### [459] [Debiasing Sequential Recommendation with Time-aware Inverse Propensity Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04986)
*Sirui Huang,Jing Long,Qian Li,Guandong Xu,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces TIPS, a new method to address selection and exposure biases in sequential recommendation systems by incorporating temporal dynamics and sequential dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper highlights biases in current sequential recommendation systems, where item exposures are overlooked, leading to selection and exposure biases in user preference modeling.

Method: The authors propose Time aware Inverse Propensity Scoring (TIPS), which goes beyond static IPS by accounting for sequential and temporal dynamics in user interactions to estimate preferences.

Result: TIPS improves performance when integrated as a plug-in with various sequential recommenders, demonstrated through extensive experiments.

Conclusion: TIPS provides an effective framework to address biases in sequential recommenders, enhancing the capability to model user preferences accurately.

Abstract: Sequential Recommendation (SR) predicts users next interactions by modeling the temporal order of their historical behaviors. Existing approaches, including traditional sequential models and generative recommenders, achieve strong performance but primarily rely on explicit interactions such as clicks or purchases while overlooking item exposures. This ignorance introduces selection bias, where exposed but unclicked items are misinterpreted as disinterest, and exposure bias, where unexposed items are treated as irrelevant. Effectively addressing these biases requires distinguishing between items that were "not exposed" and those that were "not of interest", which cannot be reliably inferred from correlations in historical data. Counterfactual reasoning provides a natural solution by estimating user preferences under hypothetical exposure, and Inverse Propensity Scoring (IPS) is a common tool for such estimation. However, conventional IPS methods are static and fail to capture the sequential dependencies and temporal dynamics of user behavior. To overcome these limitations, we propose Time aware Inverse Propensity Scoring (TIPS). Unlike traditional static IPS, TIPS effectively accounts for sequential dependencies and temporal dynamics, thereby capturing user preferences more accurately. Extensive experiments show that TIPS consistently enhances recommendation performance as a plug-in for various sequential recommenders. Our code will be publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [460] [An Approach to Simultaneous Acquisition of Real-Time MRI Video, EEG, and Surface EMG for Articulatory, Brain, and Muscle Activity During Speech Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04840)
*Jihwan Lee,Parsa Razmara,Kevin Huang,Sean Foley,Aditya Kommineni,Haley Hsu,Woojae Jeong,Prakash Kumar,Xuan Shi,Yoonjeong Lee,Tiantian Feng,Takfarinas Medani,Ye Tian,Sudarsana Reddy Kadiri,Krishna S. Nayak,Dani Byrd,Louis Goldstein,Richard M. Leahy,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel method for simultaneously collecting real-time MRI, EEG, and EMG data to study the neurophysiological mechanisms of speech production.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To gain a deeper understanding of the causal neurophysiological mechanisms behind speech production by overcoming current limitations in data collection methods.

Method: Developed a multimodal acquisition system capturing real-time MRI, EEG, and EMG during speech production, paired with an artifact suppression pipeline to mitigate MRI-induced interference and myogenic artifacts.

Result: Achieved simultaneous collection of brain signals, muscle activations, and articulatory movements, overcoming substantial technical challenges.

Conclusion: The framework offers a significant advancement for speech neuroscience and could contribute to innovations in brain-computer interface technology.

Abstract: Speech production is a complex process spanning neural planning, motor control, muscle activation, and articulatory kinematics. While the acoustic speech signal is the most accessible product of the speech production act, it does not directly reveal its causal neurophysiological substrates. We present the first simultaneous acquisition of real-time (dynamic) MRI, EEG, and surface EMG, capturing several key aspects of the speech production chain: brain signals, muscle activations, and articulatory movements. This multimodal acquisition paradigm presents substantial technical challenges, including MRI-induced electromagnetic interference and myogenic artifacts. To mitigate these, we introduce an artifact suppression pipeline tailored to this tri-modal setting. Once fully developed, this framework is poised to offer an unprecedented window into speech neuroscience and insights leading to brain-computer interface advances.

</details>


### [461] [Visual-Informed Speech Enhancement Using Attention-Based Beamforming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05270)
*Chihyun Liu,Jiaxuan Fan,Mingtung Sun,Michael Anthony,Mingsian R. Bai,Yu Tsao*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper proposes VI-NBFNet, a Visual-Informed Neural Beamforming Network, combining multimodal input features for better Speech Enhancement (SE) in challenging scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-channel SE methods struggle in challenging environments with low SNR, reverberation, dynamic speakers, or complex noise conditions.

Method: The paper introduces VI-NBFNet, integrating microphone array processing, DNNs, and lip movement features via a pretrained visual speech recognition model for VAD and speaker identification, accompanied by an attention mechanism.

Result: The experimental results showed improved SE performance and robustness for both stationary and dynamic speakers compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: VI-NBFNet effectively enhances SE in challenging scenarios, employing audiovisual features and end-to-end supervised beamforming for robustness and better performance.

Abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that incorporating auxiliary information, such as speaker voiceprint or visual cues, can substantially improve Speech Enhancement (SE) performance. However, single-channel methods often yield suboptimal results in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions, when there is high reverberation, or in complex scenarios involving dynamic speakers, overlapping speech, or non-stationary noise. To address these issues, we propose a novel Visual-Informed Neural Beamforming Network (VI-NBFNet), which integrates microphone array signal processing and deep neural networks (DNNs) using multimodal input features. The proposed network leverages a pretrained visual speech recognition model to extract lip movements as input features, which serve for voice activity detection (VAD) and target speaker identification. The system is intended to handle both static and moving speakers by introducing a supervised end-to-end beamforming framework equipped with an attention mechanism. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed audiovisual system has achieved better SE performance and robustness for both stationary and dynamic speaker scenarios, compared to several baseline methods.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [462] [Improving the accuracy of physics-informed neural networks via last-layer retraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04672)
*Saad Qadeer,Panos Stinis*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to enhance the accuracy of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) by including a post-processing step, achieving significantly lower errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the accuracy and applicability of PINNs in solving partial differential equations, as achieving suitable training strategies for them has proven challenging.

Method: A post-processing approach is employed to find the optimal approximation in a function space associated with the PINN, using basis functions and introducing a residual-based metric to determine their optimal number.

Result: The approach reduces errors by 4-5 orders of magnitude compared to standard PINNs and allows for reusing basis functions in more complex cases like time-dependent and nonlinear problems.

Conclusion: The developed method advances the performance of PINNs, offering high precision, reusability in complex scenarios, and a powerful metric for selecting basis functions effectively.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are a versatile tool in the burgeoning field of scientific machine learning for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). However, determining suitable training strategies for them is not obvious, with the result that they typically yield moderately accurate solutions. In this article, we propose a method for improving the accuracy of PINNs by coupling them with a post-processing step that seeks the best approximation in a function space associated with the network. We find that our method yields errors four to five orders of magnitude lower than those of the parent PINNs across architectures and dimensions. Moreover, we can reuse the basis functions for the linear space in more complex settings, such as time-dependent and nonlinear problems, allowing for transfer learning. Out approach also provides a residual-based metric that allows us to optimally choose the number of basis functions employed.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [463] [SarcasmMiner: A Dual-Track Post-Training Framework for Robust Audio-Visual Sarcasm Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05275)
*Zhu Li,Yongjian Chen,Huiyuan Lai,Xiyuan Gao,Shekhar Nayak,Matt Coler*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The paper introduces SarcasmMiner, a reinforcement learning framework designed for robust multimodal sarcasm detection, achieving improved F1 scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of resolving pragmatic incongruity in multimodal sarcasm detection by improving cross-modal reasoning, while resisting hallucination in foundation models.

Method: SarcasmMiner employs a reinforcement learning framework with a dual-track distillation strategy, involving a student model, high-quality teacher trajectories, and a generative reward model (GenRM) to evaluate reasoning quality. Optimization is done using group relative policy optimization (GRPO).

Result: SarcasmMiner achieves an F1 score improvement on the MUStARD++ dataset, increasing from 59.83% (zero-shot), 68.23% (supervised fine-tuning) to 70.22%.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates that reasoning-aware reward modeling can enhance multimodal performance and grounding in sarcasm detection tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection requires resolving pragmatic incongruity across textual, acoustic, and visual cues through cross-modal reasoning. To enable robust sarcasm reasoning with foundation models, we propose SarcasmMiner, a reinforcement learning based post-training framework that resists hallucination in multimodal reasoning. We reformulate sarcasm detection as structured reasoning and adopt a dual-track distillation strategy: high-quality teacher trajectories initialize the student model, while the full set of trajectories trains a generative reward model (GenRM) to evaluate reasoning quality. The student is optimized with group relative policy optimization (GRPO) using decoupled rewards for accuracy and reasoning quality. On MUStARD++, SarcasmMiner increases F1 from 59.83% (zero-shot), 68.23% (supervised finetuning) to 70.22%. These findings suggest that reasoning-aware reward modeling enhances both performance and multimodal grounding.

</details>


<div id='cs.CC'></div>

# cs.CC [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Recurrent Graph Neural Networks and Arithmetic Circuits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05140)
*Timon Barlag,Vivian Holzapfel,Laura Strieker,Jonni Virtema,Heribert Vollmer*

Main category: cs.CC

TL;DR: The paper examines the computational power of recurrent graph neural networks (GNNs) by comparing them to recurrent arithmetic circuits, establishing an equivalence in expressivity for computations over real numbers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and characterize the expressive power of recurrent GNNs by connecting them to arithmetic circuits and exploring their computational equivalence.

Method: They define recurrent arithmetic circuits as a model and construct mappings between the operations of recurrent GNNs and these circuits. The approach involves encoding labeled graph features into real numbers for GNN calculations and vice-versa.

Result: The study demonstrates that recurrent GNNs and recurrent arithmetic circuits have equivalent computational expressivity over real numbers.

Conclusion: Recurrent GNNs are as expressive as recurrent arithmetic circuits. This establishes a theoretical understanding of their computational power when dealing with labelled graph data and real-valued operations.

Abstract: We characterise the computational power of recurrent graph neural networks (GNNs) in terms of arithmetic circuits over the real numbers. Our networks are not restricted to aggregate-combine GNNs or other particular types. Generalizing similar notions from the literature, we introduce the model of recurrent arithmetic circuits, which can be seen as arithmetic analogues of sequential or logical circuits. These circuits utilise so-called memory gates which are used to store data between iterations of the recurrent circuit. While (recurrent) GNNs work on labelled graphs, we construct arithmetic circuits that obtain encoded labelled graphs as real valued tuples and then compute the same function. For the other direction we construct recurrent GNNs which are able to simulate the computations of recurrent circuits. These GNNs are given the circuit-input as initial feature vectors and then, after the GNN-computation, have the circuit-output among the feature vectors of its nodes. In this way we establish an exact correspondence between the expressivity of recurrent GNNs and recurrent arithmetic circuits operating over real numbers.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [465] [CogGen: Cognitive-Load-Informed Fully Unsupervised Deep Generative Modeling for Compressively Sampled MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04438)
*Qingyong Zhu,Yumin Tan,Xiang Gu,Dong Liang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: CogGen introduces a cognitive-load-informed strategy to improve unsupervised compressively sampled MRI (CS-MRI) by implementing staged inversion with self-paced curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve unsupervised deep generative modeling's (FU-DGM) performance in CS-MRI, which often suffers from overfitting and high compute demands in ill-conditioned inverse problems.

Method: CogGen uses staged inversion, progressively scheduling k-space weighting/selection to regulate cognitive load with self-paced curriculum learning. Early iterations focus on low-frequency data, gradually introducing high-frequency or noise-dominated data.

Result: CogGen-DIP and CogGen-INR enhance both fidelity and convergence in CS-MRI compared to unsupervised baselines and supervised pipelines in experiments.

Conclusion: CogGen effectively integrates cognitive-load-informed strategies to address challenges in unsupervised CS-MRI, demonstrating improved performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Fully unsupervised deep generative modeling (FU-DGM) is promising for compressively sampled MRI (CS-MRI) when training data or compute are limited. Classical FU-DGMs such as DIP and INR rely on architectural priors, but the ill-conditioned inverse problem often demands many iterations and easily overfits measurement noise. We propose CogGen, a cognitive-load-informed FU-DGM that casts CS-MRI as staged inversion and regulates task-side "cognitive load" by progressively scheduling intrinsic difficulty and extraneous interference. CogGen replaces uniform data fitting with an easy-to-hard k-space weighting/selection strategy: early iterations emphasize low-frequency, high-SNR, structure-dominant samples, while higher-frequency or noise-dominated measurements are introduced later. We realize this schedule via self-paced curriculum learning with complementary student-mode (what the model can currently learn) and teacher-mode (what it should follow) criteria, supporting both soft weighting and hard selection. Experiments and analysis show that CogGen-DIP and CogGen-INR improve fidelity and convergence over strong unsupervised baselines and competitive supervised pipelines.

</details>


### [466] [ICHOR: A Robust Representation Learning Approach for ASL CBF Maps with Self-Supervised Masked Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05247)
*Xavier Beltran-Urbano,Yiran Li,Xinglin Zeng,Katie R. Jobson,Manuel Taso,Christopher A. Brown,David A. Wolk,Corey T. McMillan,Ilya M. Nashrallah,Paul A. Yushkevich,Ze Wang,John A. Detre,Sudipto Dolui*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper introduces ICHOR, a novel self-supervised pre-training framework for ASL CBF maps using Vision Transformer and masked autoencoders to address challenges in ASL imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to overcome limitations in arterial spin labeling (ASL) perfusion MRI, such as variable image quality, inter-site/vendor/protocol differences, and the lack of labeled datasets for robust model generalization.

Method: ICHOR uses self-supervised learning via masked image modeling and Vision Transformer backbone, pre-training on a large dataset of 11,405 ASL CBF scans across 14 studies.

Result: ICHOR achieved superior results in ASL CBF map quality prediction and diagnostic classification tasks compared to previous neuroimaging pre-training methods.

Conclusion: This approach provides transferable representations for ASL tasks, enhancing reliability in clinical and research applications, with pre-trained weights and code openly shared for future use.

Abstract: Arterial spin labeling (ASL) perfusion MRI allows direct quantification of regional cerebral blood flow (CBF) without exogenous contrast, enabling noninvasive measurements that can be repeated without constraints imposed by contrast injection. ASL is increasingly acquired in research studies and clinical MRI protocols. Building on successes in structural imaging, recent efforts have implemented deep learning based methods to improve image quality, enable automated quality control, and derive robust quantitative and predictive biomarkers with ASL derived CBF. However, progress has been limited by variable image quality, substantial inter-site, vendor and protocol differences, and limited availability of labeled datasets needed to train models that generalize across cohorts. To address these challenges, we introduce ICHOR, a self supervised pre-training approach for ASL CBF maps that learns transferable representations using 3D masked autoencoders. ICHOR is pretrained via masked image modeling using a Vision Transformer backbone and can be used as a general-purpose encoder for downstream ASL tasks. For pre-training, we curated one of the largest ASL datasets to date, comprising 11,405 ASL CBF scans from 14 studies spanning multiple sites and acquisition protocols. We evaluated the pre-trained ICHOR encoder on three downstream diagnostic classification tasks and one ASL CBF map quality prediction regression task. Across all evaluations, ICHOR outperformed existing neuroimaging self-supervised pre-training methods adapted to ASL. Pre-trained weights and code will be made publicly available.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [467] [Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control Strategy for Alleviating Mixed Traffic Congestion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.03848)
*Zhengxuan Liu,Yuxin Cai,Yijing Wang,Xiangkun He,Chen Lv,Zhiqiang Zuo*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control (DIACC) strategy using MARL to enhance the operation of Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) in mixed traffic scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the challenge of uncertainty and diversity in Human-Driven Vehicles' (HDVs') behavior, which affects CAV cooperation in mixed traffic environments, especially in bottleneck areas of traffic flow.

Method: The DIACC strategy employs a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework and incorporates three innovations: a Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-IADM) module for local interaction perception, a Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-IEC) module for global traffic understanding, and a reward design using softmin aggregation to prioritize complex interaction scenarios. It also introduces a Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement (PSAR) module to speed up training.

Result: The proposed DIACC strategy outperforms current rule-based and MARL models by significantly enhancing traffic efficiency and adaptability in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios.

Conclusion: DIACC is an effective strategy for improving CAV cooperation in challenging mixed traffic environments by addressing the interaction between CAVs and HDVs through innovative control methods in the MARL framework.

Abstract: As Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) develops, Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) are expected to significantly reduce traffic congestion through cooperative strategies, such as in bottleneck areas. However, the uncertainty and diversity in the behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed traffic environments present major challenges for CAV cooperation. This paper proposes a Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances both local and global interaction perception within the Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios. The DIACC strategy consists of three key innovations: 1) A Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-IADM) module that enhances actor's local interaction perception by distinguishing CAV-CAV cooperative interactions from CAV-HDV observational interactions. 2) A Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-IEC) that improves critic's global traffic understanding through interaction-aware value estimation, providing more accurate guidance for policy updates. 3) A reward design that employs softmin aggregation with temperature annealing to prioritize interaction-intensive scenarios in mixed traffic. Additionally, a lightweight Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement (PSAR) module applies rule-based corrections to accelerate training convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that DIACC significantly improves traffic efficiency and adaptability compared to rule-based and benchmark MARL models.

</details>


### [468] [Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04603)
*Tichakorn Wongpiromsarn*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper proposes a formalism to evaluate system trajectories considering uncertain system-environment interactions, ensuring explainability and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of reasoning under uncertainty in system-environment interactions and managing complex relationships among differing objectives.

Method: A formalism is introduced that explicitly models the impact of system trajectories on the environment and evaluates these trajectories under the environment's responsive distribution.

Result: The formalism ensures consistency by proving a preorder on system trajectories and avoids cyclic preferences.

Conclusion: By testing on an autonomous driving example, the formalism demonstrates improved explainability in trajectory selection, validating its utility.

Abstract: We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions between the system and its environment. The proposed formalism supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically handles complex relationships among requirements and objectives, including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly model how each system trajectory influences the environment and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the approach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the rationale behind trajectory selection.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.PM'></div>

# q-fin.PM [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [Explainable Regime Aware Investing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04441)
*Amine Boukardagha*

Main category: q-fin.PM

TL;DR: The paper introduces an explainable regime-aware portfolio construction method using a Wasserstein Hidden Markov Model for adapting portfolio strategies while maintaining economic interpretability and achieving superior financial performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to solve the issue of constructing portfolios that are both explainable and adaptive to changing financial regimes, improving risk-adjusted returns and stabilizing performance during market shifts.

Method: The method involves a Wasserstein Hidden Markov Model (HMM) with 2-Wasserstein distance for dynamic regime inference, integrated with a mean-variance optimization framework that is aware of transaction costs.

Result: The proposed model outperformed benchmarks with a Sharpe ratio of 2.18 compared to 1.59 and 1.18, achieved a lower drawdown of -5.43%, and showed robustness during market stress scenarios like the Liberation Day selloff.

Conclusion: Regime stability and adaptive complexity management are crucial for improving portfolio robustness, making the Wasserstein HMM framework effective for real-world daily asset allocation.

Abstract: We propose an explainable regime-aware portfolio construction framework based on a strictly causal Wasserstein Hidden Markov Model. The model combines rolling Gaussian HMM inference with predictive model-order selection and template-based identity tracking using the 2-Wasserstein distance between Gaussian components. This allows regime complexity to adapt dynamically while preserving stable economic interpretation. Regime probabilities are embedded into a transaction-cost-aware mean-variance optimization framework and evaluated on a diversified daily cross-asset universe. Relative to equal-weight and SPX buy-and-hold benchmarks, the Wasserstein HMM achieves materially higher risk-adjusted performance with Sharpe ratios of 2.18 versus 1.59 and 1.18 and substantially lower maximum drawdown of negative 5.43 percent versus negative 14.62 percent for SPX. During the early 2025 equity selloff labeled Liberation Day, the strategy dynamically reduced equity exposure and shifted toward defensive assets, mitigating peak-to-trough losses. Compared to a nonparametric KNN conditional-moment estimator using the same features and optimization layer, the parametric regime model produces materially lower turnover and smoother weight evolution. The results demonstrate that regime inference stability, particularly identity preservation and adaptive complexity control, is a first-order determinant of portfolio drawdown and implementation robustness in daily asset allocation.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [470] [Benchmark of Benchmarks: Unpacking Influence and Code Repository Quality in LLM Safety Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04459)
*Junjie Chu,Xinyue Shen,Ye Leng,Michael Backes,Yun Shen,Yang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the influence and code quality of 31 LLM safety benchmarks, finding no significant academic advantage over non-benchmark papers, and highlights major gaps in code quality and ethical considerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in tracking and evaluating progress in LLM safety research due to its rapid growth.

Method: The paper conducts a multi-dimensional evaluation of the academic influence (using five metrics) and code quality (using automated and human assessments) of LLM safety benchmarks and analysis of 31 benchmarks and 382 non-benchmark papers.

Result: Benchmark papers showed no significant academic influence advantage, major misalignments correlate author prominence to influence, while code quality remains poor with issues in usability, installation guides, and ethics.

Conclusion: Prominent researchers must set higher standards for benchmarks to ensure better usability, transparent practices, and ethical considerations in LLM safety research.

Abstract: The rapid growth of research in LLM safety makes it hard to track all advances. Benchmarks are therefore crucial for capturing key trends and enabling systematic comparisons. Yet, it remains unclear why certain benchmarks gain prominence, and no systematic assessment has been conducted on their academic influence or code quality. This paper fills this gap by presenting the first multi-dimensional evaluation of the influence (based on five metrics) and code quality (based on both automated and human assessment) on LLM safety benchmarks, analyzing 31 benchmarks and 382 non-benchmarks across prompt injection, jailbreak, and hallucination. We find that benchmark papers show no significant advantage in academic influence (e.g., citation count and density) over non-benchmark papers. We uncover a key misalignment: while author prominence correlates with paper influence, neither author prominence nor paper influence shows a significant correlation with code quality. Our results also indicate substantial room for improvement in code and supplementary materials: only 39% of repositories are ready-to-use, 16% include flawless installation guides, and a mere 6% address ethical considerations. Given that the work of prominent researchers tends to attract greater attention, they need to lead the effort in setting higher standards.

</details>


### [471] [Osmosis Distillation: Model Hijacking with the Fewest Samples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04859)
*Yuchen Shi,Huajie Chen,Heng Xu,Zhiquan Liu,Jialiang Shen,Chi Liu,Shuai Zhou,Tianqing Zhu,Wanlei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces 'Osmosis Distillation (OD)', demonstrating a security vulnerability in transfer learning with synthetic datasets, where an adversary can hijack models using very few poisoned samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore potential security threats when using synthetic datasets generated by dataset distillation in transfer learning, addressing how adversaries can exploit this vulnerability.

Method: The authors propose the 'Osmosis Distillation (OD)' attack, a model hijacking technique that uses minimal poisoned samples to manipulate deep learning models during transfer learning.

Result: The OD attack achieves high success rates in hidden tasks while maintaining good utility in original tasks across various datasets and architectures.

Conclusion: The study highlights a significant security risk in transfer learning with synthetic datasets, urging caution and awareness when using such datasets, especially those from third-party sources.

Abstract: Transfer learning is devised to leverage knowledge from pre-trained models to solve new tasks with limited data and computational resources. Meanwhile, dataset distillation has emerged to synthesize a compact dataset that preserves critical information from the original large dataset. Therefore, a combination of transfer learning and dataset distillation offers promising performance in evaluations. However, a non-negligible security threat remains undiscovered in transfer learning using synthetic datasets generated by dataset distillation methods, where an adversary can perform a model hijacking attack with only a few poisoned samples in the synthetic dataset. To reveal this threat, we propose Osmosis Distillation (OD) attack, a novel model hijacking strategy that targets deep learning models using the fewest samples. Comprehensive evaluations on various datasets demonstrate that the OD attack attains high attack success rates in hidden tasks while preserving high model utility in original tasks. Furthermore, the distilled osmosis set enables model hijacking across diverse model architectures, allowing model hijacking in transfer learning with considerable attack performance and model utility. We argue that awareness of using third-party synthetic datasets in transfer learning must be raised.

</details>


### [472] [AgentSCOPE: Evaluating Contextual Privacy Across Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04902)
*Ivoline C. Ngong,Keerthiram Murugesan,Swanand Kadhe,Justin D. Weisz,Amit Dhurandhar,Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Agentic systems often overlook intermediate privacy risks within information flows. Privacy Flow Graph helps assess these flows independently, revealing privacy concerns in large language model pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Highlighting the potential privacy violations in agentic systems during intermediate information flows and addressing the shortcomings of focusing solely on input/output evaluation.

Method: Developed the Privacy Flow Graph framework based on Contextual Integrity to map and analyze intermediate information flows, complemented by the AgentSCOPE benchmark for assessing privacy risks across scenarios.

Result: Privacy violations occur in over 80% of scenarios in LLM pipelines, primarily at the tool-response stage with indiscriminate API data sharing.

Conclusion: Intermediate privacy risks in agentic systems are critical and neglected, necessitating robust evaluation frameworks to ensure comprehensive privacy protection.

Abstract: Agentic systems are increasingly acting on users' behalf, accessing calendars, email, and personal files to complete everyday tasks. Privacy evaluation for these systems has focused on the input and output boundaries, but each task involves several intermediate information flows, from agent queries to tool responses, that are not currently evaluated. We argue that every boundary in an agentic pipeline is a site of potential privacy violation and must be assessed independently. To support this, we introduce the Privacy Flow Graph, a Contextual Integrity-grounded framework that decomposes agentic execution into a sequence of information flows, each annotated with the five CI parameters, and traces violations to their point of origin. We present AgentSCOPE, a benchmark of 62 multi-tool scenarios across eight regulatory domains with ground truth at every pipeline stage. Our evaluation across seven state-of-the-art LLMs show that privacy violations in the pipeline occur in over 80% of scenarios, even when final outputs appear clean (24%), with most violations arising at the tool-response stage where APIs return sensitive data indiscriminately. These results indicate that output-level evaluation alone substantially underestimates the privacy risk of agentic systems.

</details>


### [473] [Good-Enough LLM Obfuscation (GELO)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05035)
*Anatoly Belikov,Ilya Fedotov*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: GELO is a lightweight protocol that safeguards prompt privacy for open-source LLMs on shared accelerators by obfuscating hidden states with batch-specific random mixing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the lack of privacy protections for prompt data in open-source LLMs when deployed on shared accelerators, where adversaries can exploit observable KV caches and hidden states.

Method: GELO uses a per-batch invertible mixing protocol, where a random matrix is applied to hidden states during offloading, and its inverse is applied upon return, preserving outputs while protecting privacy.

Result: GELO achieves effectiveness on Llama-2 7B, maintaining output accuracy, operating with manageable latency overheads (20-30%), and resisting advanced cryptanalysis attacks.

Conclusion: GELO provides a practical, privacy-preserving solution for LLM inference on shared accelerators without significant computational trade-offs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly served on shared accelerators where an adversary with read access to device memory can observe KV caches and hidden states, threatening prompt privacy for open-source models. Cryptographic protections such as MPC and FHE offer strong guarantees but remain one to two orders of magnitude too slow for interactive inference, while static obfuscation schemes break under multi-run statistical attacks once the model is known. We present GELO (Good-Enough LLM Obfuscation), a lightweight protocol for privacy-preserving inference that limits information leakage from untrusted accelerator observations by hiding hidden states with fresh, per-batch invertible mixing. For each offloaded projection, the TEE samples a random matrix A, forms $U = AH$, offloads U and weights W to the accelerator, and then applies $A^-1$ on return, so that $A^-1 ((AH)W ) = HW$ and outputs are unchanged. Because mixing is never reused across batches, the attacker faces only a single-batch blind source separation problem. We analyze information leakage and introduce two practical defenses: (i) non-orthogonal mixing to mask Gram matrices, and (ii) orthogonal mixing augmented with a small fraction of high-energy "shield" vectors that pollute higher-order statistics. On Llama-2 7B, GELO preserves float32 outputs exactly, closely matches low-precision baselines, offloads the dominant matrix multiplications with about 20-30% latency overhead, and defeats a range of ICA/BSS and anchor-based attacks.

</details>


### [474] [Cyber Threat Intelligence for Artificial Intelligence Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05068)
*Natalia Krawczyk,Mateusz Szczepkowski,Adrian Brodzik,Krzysztof Bocianiak*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper examines evolving cyber threat intelligence (CTI) to protect AI systems, reveals security gaps in existing tools, and proposes a tailored knowledge base for AI-oriented defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increased integration of AI into services and products makes it vulnerable to novel security risks that traditional defenses cannot address effectively.

Method: The paper analyzes conventional CTI workflows, reviews AI-specific vulnerabilities, organizes AI security knowledge, and proposes an AI-oriented threat intelligence framework.

Result: It identifies AI-specific vulnerabilities, organizes IoC for AI systems, and highlights deficiencies in current CTI tools and frameworks.

Conclusion: The research emphasizes the need for future work to create robust threat intelligence tailored to the unique security demands of AI systems.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes deeply embedded in critical services and everyday products, it is increasingly exposed to security threats which traditional cyber defenses were not designed to handle. In this paper, we investigate how cyber threat intelligence (CTI) may evolve to address attacks that target AI systems. We first analyze the assumptions and workflows of conventional threat intelligence with the needs of AI-focused defense, highlighting AI-specific assets and vulnerabilities. We then review and organize the current landscape of AI security knowledge. Based on this, we outline what an AI-oriented threat intelligence knowledge base should contain, describing concrete indicators of compromise (IoC) for different AI supply-chain phases and artifacts, and showing how such a knowledge base could support security tools. Finally, we discuss techniques for measuring similarity between collected indicators and newly observed AI artifacts. The review reveals gaps and quality issues in existing resources and identifies potential future research directions toward a practical threat intelligence framework tailored to AI.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [475] [FluxSieve: Unifying Streaming and Analytical Data Planes for Scalable Cloud Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04937)
*Adriano Vogel,Sören Henning,Otmar Ertl*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: FluxSieve proposes an architecture integrating in-stream precomputation into data ingestion to enhance query performance by merging pull-based and push-based processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern data platforms struggle under high concurrency and intensive queries, a challenge magnified in large-scale observability systems due to recurring, costly filtering queries at runtime.

Method: FluxSieve introduces an in-stream filtering layer, integrates it into the ingestion process, unifies streaming/analytical data planes, implements scalable pattern matching, and showcases its integration with systems like Apache Pinot and DuckDB.

Result: The proposed method achieves orders-of-magnitude improvements in query performance, with minimal storage cost and computational overhead, validated through extensive experiments.

Conclusion: FluxSieve effectively addresses inefficiencies in query and computational overhead by unifying streaming and analytical data planes, proving its utility and scalability across various platforms.

Abstract: Despite many advances in query optimization, indexing techniques, and data storage, modern data platforms still face difficulties in delivering robust query performance under high concurrency and computationally intensive queries. This challenge is particularly pronounced in large-scale observability platforms handling high-volume, high-velocity data records. For instance, recurrent, expensive filtering queries at query time impose substantial computational and storage overheads in the analytical data plane. In this paper, we propose FluxSieve, a unified architecture that reconciles traditional pull-based query processing with push-based stream processing by embedding a lightweight in-stream precomputation and filtering layer directly into the data ingestion path. This avoids the complexity and operational burden of running queries in dedicated stream processing frameworks. Concretely, this work (i) introduces a foundational architecture that unifies streaming and analytical data planes via in-stream filtering and records enrichment, (ii) designs a scalable multi-pattern matching mechanism that supports concurrent evaluation and on-the-fly updates of filtering rules with minimal per-record overhead, (iii) demonstrates how to integrate this ingestion-time processing with two open-source analytical systems -- Apache Pinot as a Real-Time Online Analytical Processing (RTOLAP) engine and DuckDB as an embedded analytical database, and (iv) performs comprehensive experimental evaluation of our approach. Our evaluation across different systems, query types, and performance metrics shows up to orders-of-magnitude improvements in query performance at the cost of negligible additional storage and very low computational overhead.

</details>


### [476] [Beyond Linear LLM Invocation: An Efficient and Effective Semantic Filter Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04799)
*Nan Hou,Kangfei Zhao,Jiadong Xie,Jeffrey Xu Yu*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces Clustering-Sampling-Voting (CSV), a framework to optimize semantic filtering with large language models (LLMs), achieving sublinear query complexity and maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiencies in semantic filtering with LLMs, where linear scanning of data incurs high latency and costs in managing large-scale queries.

Method: Proposes CSV framework involving tuple embedding into semantic clusters, sampling a subset for LLM evaluation, and cluster-level label inference using two voting strategies: UniVote and SimVote. Incorporates re-clustering for robustness.

Result: CSV reduces LLM invocations by factors ranging from 1.28x to 355x compared to current methods while maintaining accuracy metrics like Accuracy and F1 score on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: CSV demonstrates an efficient and scalable approach for semantic query processing using LLMs, overcoming previous linear invocation limitations with minimal trade-offs in effectiveness.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for semantic query processing over large corpora. A set of semantic operators derived from relational algebra has been proposed to provide a unified interface for expressing such queries, among which the semantic filter operator serves as a cornerstone. Given a table T with a natural language predicate e, for each tuple in the relation, the execution of a semantic filter proceeds by constructing an input prompt that combines the predicate e with its content, querying the LLM, and obtaining the binary decision. However, this tuple-by-tuple evaluation necessitates a complete linear scan of the table, incurring prohibitive latency and token costs. Although recent work has attempted to optimize semantic filtering, it still does not break the linear LLM invocation barriers. To address this, we propose Clustering-Sampling-Voting (CSV), a new framework that reduces LLM invocations to sublinear complexity while providing error guarantees. CSV embeds tuples into semantic clusters, samples a small subset for LLM evaluation, and infers cluster-level labels via two proposed voting strategies: UniVote, which aggregates labels uniformly, and SimVote, which weights votes by semantic similarity. Moreover, CSV triggers re-clustering on ambiguous clusters to ensure robustness across diverse datasets. The results conducted on real-world datasets demonstrate that CSV reduces the number of LLM calls by 1.28-355x compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, while maintaining comparable effectiveness in terms of Accuracy and F1 score.

</details>


### [477] [Deterministic Preprocessing and Interpretable Fuzzy Banding for Cost-per-Student Reporting from Extracted Records](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04905)
*Shane Lee,Stella Ng*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: This paper discusses a file-based workflow for processing a Casual Academic Database export into various summarized sheets, with deterministic methods for ensuring reproducibility and a fuzzy banding system for interpreting cost-per-student ratios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a systematic, reproducible method for analyzing data from casual academic databases, ensuring accuracy in budgeting and workload decisions.

Method: The study implements a deterministic Python script, cad_processor.py, processing input workbooks to generate aggregated data and summaries with clear computational processes, including hashed snapshot verification and fuzzy band labels.

Result: The workflow produces a processed workbook containing summaries, cost analyses, and fuzzy band categorizations, supported by reproducible calculations and supplementary explanatory materials.

Conclusion: This approach provides a robust, transparent, and reproducible method for analyzing administrative extracts, aiding decision-making in academic governance.

Abstract: Administrative extracts are often exchanged as spreadsheets and may be read as reports in their own right during budgeting, workload review, and governance discussions. When an exported workbook becomes the reference snapshot for such decisions, the transformation can be checked by recomputation against a clearly identified input.
  A deterministic, rule-governed, file-based workflow is implemented in cad_processor.py. The script ingests a Casual Academic Database (CAD) export workbook and aggregates inclusive on-costs and student counts into subject-year and school-year totals, from which it derives cost-per-student ratios. It writes a processed workbook with four sheets: Processing Summary (run record and counters), Trend Analysis (schoolyear cost-per-student matrix), Report (wide subject-level table), and Fuzzy Bands (per-year anchors, membership weights, and band labels). The run record includes a SHA-256 hash of the input workbook bytes to support snapshot-matched recomputation.
  For within-year interpretation, the workflow adds a simple fuzzy banding layer that labels finite, positive school-year cost-per-student values as Low, Medium, or High. The per-year anchors are the minimum, median, and maximum of the finite, positive ratios. Membership weights are computed using left-shoulder, triangular, and right-shoulder functions, with deterministic tie-breaking in a fixed priority order (Medium, then Low, then High). These weights are treated as decision-support signals rather than probabilities.
  A worked example provides a reproducible calculation of a band assignment from the reported anchors and ratios. Supplementary material includes a claim-to-evidence matrix, a reproducibility note, and a short glossary that links selected statements to code and workbook artefacts.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [478] [MPBMC: Multi-Property Bounded Model Checking with GNN-guided Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04450)
*Soumik Guha Roy,Sumana Ghosh,Ansuman Banerjee,Raj Kumar Gajavelly,Sudhakar Surendran*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper proposes using graph neural networks to cluster properties for efficient multi-property verification, improving bounded model checking performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in efficiently clustering properties for multi-property verification to enhance formal verification processes.

Method: Combining graph neural network embeddings with runtime design statistics for creating functional property clusters.

Result: The proposed hybrid approach demonstrated verification speedup and effective clustering in HWMCC benchmarks.

Conclusion: Utilizing GNN embeddings and design statistics can significantly improve multi-property verification speed and efficiency.

Abstract: Formal verification of designs with multiple properties has been a long-standing challenge for the verification research community. The task of coming up with an effective strategy that can efficiently cluster properties to be solved together has inspired a number of proposals, ranging from structural clustering based on the property cone of influence (COI) to leverage runtime design and verification statistics. In this paper, we present an attempt towards functional clustering of properties utilizing graph neural network (GNN) embeddings for creating effective property clusters. We propose a hybrid approach that can exploit neural functional representations of hardware circuits and runtime design statistics to speed up the performance of Bounded Model Checking (BMC) in the context of multi-property verification (MPV). Our method intelligently groups properties based on their functional embedding and design statistics, resulting in speedup in verification results. Experimental results on the HWMCC benchmarks show the efficacy of our proposal with respect to the state-of-the-art.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [479] [Auto-Generating Personas from User Reviews in VR App Stores](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04985)
*Yi Wang,Kexin Cheng,Xiao Liu,Chetan Arora,John Grundy,Thuong Hoang,Henry Been-Lirn Duh*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Auto-generated personas were used in VR education to enhance accessibility-focused discussions and design, fostering efficient empathy development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited use of personas in eliciting accessibility requirements in VR projects and overcome challenges in applying them effectively.

Method: Implementation of an auto-generated persona system in VR courses to facilitate discussions and improve accessibility in VR design and development.

Result: Students developed empathy more efficiently using the auto-generated personas, enhancing discussions on accessibility requirements.

Conclusion: Automatically generated personas can successfully elicit latent accessibility requirements and promote empathy in VR design within educational settings.

Abstract: Personas are a valuable tool for discussing accessibility requirements in software design and development practices. However, the use of personas for accessibility-focused requirements elicitation in VR projects remains limited and is accompanied by several challenges. To fill this gap, we developed an auto-generated persona system in a VR course, where the personas were used to facilitate discussions on accessibility requirements and to guide VR design and development. Our findings indicate that the auto-generated persona system enabled students to develop empathy more efficiently. This study demonstrates the use of automatically generated personas in VR course settings as a means of eliciting latent accessibility requirements.

</details>


### [480] [How Professional Visual Artists are Negotiating Generative AI in the Workplace](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04537)
*Harry H. Jiang,Jordan Taylor,William Agnew*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper surveys 378 professional visual artists and finds overwhelming opposition to generative AI, citing stress and reduced career opportunities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the professional impacts of generative AI on visual artists' workplaces and careers.

Method: Survey of 378 verified professional visual artists to assess generative AI's effects on their careers and workplaces.

Result: Artists overwhelmingly oppose generative AI, employ refusal strategies, experience pressure from workplace dynamics, and report negative impacts like stress and diminished opportunities.

Conclusion: HCI researchers should prioritize artists’ preferences to avoid using generative AI and address the challenges faced by professionals in these contexts.

Abstract: Generative AI has been heavily critiqued by artists in both popular media and HCI scholarship. However, more work is needed to understand the impacts of generative AI on professional artists' workplaces and careers. In this paper, we conduct a survey of \textit{378 verified professional visual artists} about how generative AI has impacted their careers and workplaces. We find (1) most visual artists are strongly opposed to using generative AI (text or visual) and negotiate their inclusion in the workplace through a variety of \textit{refusal} strategies (2) there exist a range of factors in artists environments shaping their use of generative AI, including pressure from clients, bosses, and peers and (3) visual artists report overwhelmingly negative impacts of generative AI on their workplaces, leading to added stress and reduced job opportunities. In light of these findings, we encourage HCI researchers to contend more deeply with artists' desires not to use generative AI in the workplace.

</details>


### [481] [Not All Trust is the Same: Effects of Decision Workflow and Explanations in Human-AI Decision Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05229)
*Laura Spillner,Rachel Ringe,Robert Porzel,Rainer Malaka*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how decision workflows, explanations, and user factors influence trust and behavior in AI-assisted decision-making, finding nuances in trust measurement and interactions with user knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenge of achieving balanced trust in AI-assisted decision-making, avoiding both overtrust and undertrust, while exploring the impact of decision workflows and explanations.

Method: The researchers conducted experiments varying decision workflows (1-step vs. 2-step), presence of explanations, and evaluating user characteristics like domain knowledge and AI experience, comparing self-reported trust and reliance behaviors.

Result: They found no evidence that a 2-step workflow reduces overreliance or affects self-reported trust directly, but observed interaction effects with domain knowledge and explanation availability.

Conclusion: Reported trust and reliance behaviors are distinct and should be separately evaluated in AI-assisted decision-making, with design impacts varying based on user knowledge and workflow setup.

Abstract: A central challenge in AI-assisted decision making is achieving warranted, well-calibrated trust. Both overtrust (accepting incorrect AI recommendations) and undertrust (rejecting correct advice) should be prevented. Prior studies differ in the design of the decision workflow - whether users see the AI suggestion immediately (1-step setup) or have to submit a first decision beforehand (2-step setup) -, and in how trust is measured - through self-reports or as behavioral trust, that is, reliance. We examined the effects and interactions of (a) the type of decision workflow, (b) the presence of explanations, and (c) users' domain knowledge and prior AI experience. We compared reported trust, reliance (agreement rate and switch rate), and overreliance. Results showed no evidence that a 2-step setup reduces overreliance. The decision workflow also did not directly affect self-reported trust, but there was a crossover interaction effect with domain knowledge and explanations, suggesting that the effects of explanations alone may not generalize across workflow setups. Finally, our findings confirm that reported trust and reliance behavior are distinct constructs that should be evaluated separately in AI-assisted decision making.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [482] [When Denoising Hinders: Revisiting Zero-Shot ASR with SAM-Audio and Whisper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04710)
*Akif Islam,Raufun Nahar,Md. Ekramul Hamid*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This study investigates the effect of speech enhancement using SAM-Audio on the performance of zero-shot ASR systems like Whisper. Contrary to expectations, SAM-Audio preprocessing degrades ASR outcomes despite improving audio quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research challenges the assumption that enhancing perceptual audio quality using advanced models like SAM-Audio automatically improves ASR accuracy in noisy environments.

Method: An empirical study was conducted using SAM-Audio preprocessing for Whisper ASR on Bengali and English noisy datasets across various Whisper model variants. Performance was measured via metrics like WER and CER, alongside acoustic quality analysis.

Result: The results indicate that SAM-Audio preprocessing consistently degrades ASR performance, with increased WER and CER, despite cleaner perceived audio. Larger Whisper models performed worse after SAM-Audio preprocessing.

Conclusion: While SAM-Audio improves human-perceived audio quality, it introduces systematic recognition issues for machines. This suggests that advanced denoising should not be blindly integrated into zero-shot ASR systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech enhancement have led to a widespread assumption that improving perceptual audio quality should directly benefit recognition accuracy. In this work, we rigorously examine whether this assumption holds for modern zero-shot ASR systems. We present a systematic empirical study on the impact of Segment Anything Model Audio by Meta AI, a recent foundation-scale speech enhancement model proposed by Meta, when used as a preprocessing step for zero-shot transcription with Whisper. Experiments are conducted across multiple Whisper model variants and two linguistically distinct noisy speech datasets: a real-world Bengali YouTube corpus and a publicly available English noisy dataset. Contrary to common intuition, our results show that SAM-Audio preprocessing consistently degrades ASR performance, increasing both Word Error Rate (WER) and Character Error Rate (CER) compared to raw noisy speech, despite substantial improvements in signal-level quality. Objective Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio analysis on the English dataset confirms that SAM-Audio produces acoustically cleaner signals, yet this improvement fails to translate into recognition gains. Therefore, we conducted a detailed utterance-level analysis to understand this counterintuitive result. We found that the recognition degradation is a systematic issue affecting the majority of the audio, not just isolated outliers, and that the errors worsen as the Whisper model size increases. These findings expose a fundamental mismatch: audio that is perceptually cleaner to human listeners is not necessarily robust for machine recognition. This highlights the risk of blindly applying state-of-the-art denoising as a preprocessing step in zero-shot ASR pipelines.

</details>


### [483] [WhisperAlign: Word-Boundary-Aware ASR and WhisperX-Anchored Pyannote Diarization for Long-Form Bengali Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04809)
*Aurchi Chowdhury,Rubaiyat -E-Zaman,Sk. Ashrafuzzaman Nafees*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper develops solutions for Bengali Long-Form Speech Recognition and Speaker Diarization challenges using targeted audio chunking and domain-specific model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address transcription accuracy issues and speaker diarization errors due to challenges like overlapping speech and long-form Bengali audio in low-resource settings.

Method: Used a precise chunking strategy with Whisper timestamping for transcription and fine-tuned Pyannote.audio on the competition dataset for Bengali conversational diarization.

Result: The methods significantly improved Word Error Rate (WER) and Diarization Error Rate (DER).

Conclusion: Targeted chunking and domain-specific fine-tuning effectively resolve complex transcription and speaker boundaries in Bengali multi-speaker audio tasks.

Abstract: This paper presents our solution for the DL Sprint 4.0, addressing the dual challenges of Bengali Long-Form Speech Recognition (Task 1) and Speaker Diarization (Task 2). Processing long-form, multi-speaker Bengali audio introduces significant hurdles in voice activity detection, overlapping speech, and context preservation. To solve the long-form transcription challenge, we implemented a robust audio chunking strategy utilizing whisper-timestamped, allowing us to feed precise, context-aware segments into our fine-tuned acoustic model for high-accuracy transcription. For the diarization task, we developed an integrated pipeline leveraging pyannote.audio and WhisperX. A key contribution of our approach is the domain-specific fine-tuning of the Pyannote segmentation model on the competition dataset. This adaptation allowed the model to better capture the nuances of Bengali conversational dynamics and accurately resolve complex, overlapping speaker boundaries. Our methodology demonstrates that applying intelligent timestamped chunking to ASR and targeted segmentation fine-tuning to diarization significantly drives down Word Error Rate (WER) and Diarization Error Rate (DER), in low-resource settings.

</details>


### [484] [Boosting ASR Robustness via Test-Time Reinforcement Learning with Audio-Text Semantic Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05231)
*Linghan Fang,Tianxin Xie,Li Liu*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper proposes ASR-TRA, a novel Test-time Reinforcement Adaptation method for improving Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system performance on challenging real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sensitivity of ASR systems to data distribution shifts (e.g., noise and accents) and mitigate issues with current test-time adaptation methods such as confirmation bias.

Method: The ASR-TRA framework introduces a learnable decoder prompt and stochastic decoding, employing reinforcement learning with a reward model to align audio and text semantically.

Result: ASR-TRA outperforms test-time adaptation baselines in terms of accuracy and latency on noisy LibriSpeech and L2 Arctic datasets.

Conclusion: ASR-TRA is a practical and robust solution for deploying ASR in real-world noisy and accented environments, offering enhanced adaptability and interpretability.

Abstract: Recently, Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems (e.g., Whisper) have achieved remarkable accuracy improvements but remain highly sensitive to real-world unseen data (data with large distribution shifts), including noisy environments and diverse accents. To address this issue, test-time adaptation (TTA) has shown great potential in improving the model adaptability at inference time without ground-truth labels, and existing TTA methods often rely on pseudo-labeling or entropy minimization. However, by treating model confidence as a learning signal, these methods may reinforce high-confidence errors, leading to confirmation bias that undermines adaptation. To overcome these limitations, we present ASR-TRA, a novel Test-time Reinforcement Adaptation framework inspired by causal intervention. More precisely, our method introduces a learnable decoder prompt and utilizes temperature-controlled stochastic decoding to generate diverse transcription candidates. These are scored by a reward model that measures audio-text semantic alignment, and the resulting feedback is used to update both model and prompt parameters via reinforcement learning. Comprehensive experiments on LibriSpeech with synthetic noise and L2 Arctic accented English datasets demonstrate that our method achieves higher accuracy while maintaining lower latency than existing TTA baselines. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of combining audio and language-based rewards, highlighting our method's enhanced stability and interpretability. Overall, our approach provides a practical and robust solution for deploying ASR systems in challenging real-world conditions.

</details>


### [485] [Latent-Mark: An Audio Watermark Robust to Neural Resynthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05310)
*Yen-Shan Chen,Shih-Yu Lai,Ying-Jung Tsou,Yi-Cheng Lin,Bing-Yu Chen,Yun-Nung Chen,Hung-Yi Lee,Shang-Tse Chen*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper introduces Latent-Mark, a zero-bit audio watermarking method that survives semantic compression by targeting codec latent spaces, achieving robustness against DSP attacks and neural codec resynthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audio watermarking techniques struggle with neural resynthesis due to modern codecs acting as semantic filters, discarding imperceptible information embedded in waveforms.

Method: The authors propose embedding watermarks in neural audio codecs' invariant latent space using optimized waveform perturbations aligned with natural audio manifold and cross-codec optimization to achieve robustness.

Result: Latent-Mark shows robust transferability across unseen neural codecs and state-of-the-art resilience against DSP attacks, maintaining perceptual imperceptibility.
